text:
This study examined the growth, implications and challenges of Theological Education in The Apostolic Church Nigeria (TACN) since its founding in 1931. Generally, in Pentecostal settings, formal education is subservient to spiritual experiences of the Holy Ghost. In the case of the TACN this study investigated how this trend was addressed in the Church in a move to ensure doctrinal integrity and training of Pastors through the adoption of formal education as a means of empowering them spiritually, academically, professionally, physically, socially morally and culturally. This study therefore examined the development of Theological Education within TACN from 1931 to the present time. It adopted historical and qualitative approaches to explore the trend within the Church, using both literary and oral data obtained from source materials such as journals and textbooks among other information obtained through in-depth interviews with academics, graduates and students of the TACN Bible Colleges nationwide.

The study established that the impact of TACN’s investment in institutions, such as the LAWNA Theological Seminaries in Ilesa and Jos and the Theological Seminary in Amumara, had raised the level of scholarly and spiritual competence of the leadership of the independent churches and enhanced their structures and doctrines. However, it also identified a number of challenges facing the independent churches including financial strains, poor infrastructure and cultural bias against formal learning in favour of “spiritual spontaneity”.

This study evaluates the relevance of the TACN as an educational institution in a modern era and points to the necessity of infrastructure and technology development as well as e-learning and faculty development in maintaining a contemporary pertinence of the organization. The authors propose an educational model that integrates intellectual academicity and a strong focus on the spiritual growth of students. The study presents a comprehensive historical analysis of pedagogical concepts that make up the ideology of the TACN. It offers a rich case-study analysis on the intricacies of imparting formal theological education within the context of a thriving and progressive religious movement similar to that of Pentecostalism and therefore opens a new avenue for future empirical research regarding similar Pentecostal religious movements that struggle to ensure an adequate bridge between traditional practices and more contemporary educational standards and standards of theoretical and academic pedagogy.
